prompt: I'm new to event-driven architecture and want a 30-minute intro that's not a vendor pitch.

id_to_title.doc_10008f: Roo Code: A Guide With 7 Practical Examples

excerpts.purpose: Defines the core reliability problem with publishing messages alongside DB updates and why a special pattern is needed.
excerpts.excerpt:
A service command typically needs to create/update/delete [aggregates] in the database **and** send messages/events to a message broker. For example, a service that participates in a [saga] needs to update business entities and send messages/events. Similarly, a service that publishes a [domain event] must update an [aggregate] and publish an event.

The command must atomically update the database and send messages in order to avoid data inconsistencies and bugs.

excerpts.purpose: Explains the transactional outbox mechanism used to make message publishing reliable without 2PC.
excerpts.excerpt:
The solution is for the service that sends the message to first store the message in the database as part of the transaction that updates the business entities. A separate process then sends the messages to the message broker.

The participants in this pattern are:

*   Sender - the service that sends the message

excerpts.purpose: Highlights the consequence of retries/duplicate delivery and the requirement that consumers handle idempotency.
excerpts.excerpt: The Message relay might publish a message more than once. It might, for example, crash after publishing a message but before recording the fact that it has done so. When it restarts, it will then publish the message again. As a result, a message consumer must be idempotent, perhaps by tracking the IDs of the messages that it has already processed.

excerpts.purpose: Provides a simple definition of a saga and how it achieves cross-service consistency via compensation.
excerpts.excerpt: Implement each business transaction that spans multiple services as a saga. A saga is a sequence of local transactions. Each local transaction updates the database and publishes a message or event to trigger the next local transaction in the saga. If a local transaction fails because it violates a business rule then the saga executes a series of compensating transactions that undo the changes that were made by the preceding local transactions.

excerpts.purpose: Summarizes two coordination styles for sagas (choreography vs orchestration).
excerpts.excerpt:
There are two ways of coordination sagas:

*   Choreography - each local transaction publishes domain events that trigger local transactions in other services
*   Orchestration - an orchestrator (object) tells the participants what local transactions to execute

excerpts.purpose: Introduces event sourcing as persisting state changes as an event log that can rebuild past state.
excerpts.excerpt:
Event Sourcing ensures that all changes to application state are stored as a sequence of events. Not just can we query these events, we can also use the event log to reconstruct past states, and as a foundation to automatically adjust the state to cope with retroactive changes.

The fundamental idea of Event Sourcing is that of ensuring every change to the state of an application is captured in an event object, and that these event objects are themselves stored in the sequence they were applied

excerpts.purpose: Gives a neutral “what is Kafka” mental model: durable event log with topics and replay by consumers.
excerpts.excerpt:
Kafka is a distributed _event streaming platform_ that lets you read, write, store, and process [_events_](https://kafka.apache.org/documentation/#messages) (also called _records_ or _messages_ in the documentation) across many machines.

A Kafka client communicates with the Kafka brokers via the network for writing (or reading) events. Once received, the brokers will store the events in a durable and fault-tolerant manner for as long as you need—even forever.

further_reading.blurb: Useful if you’ll use an async job queue in addition to event streams—covers practical guidance like keeping job parameters small and handling retries safely.
Good reinforcement for the general idea of idempotency keys—how clients avoid harmful effects from repeating requests.
A classic “what to avoid” reference; helps you recognize how distributed systems can become tangled without deliberate architectural structure.

prompt: We're picking between Postgres SKIP LOCKED and a managed SQS for a 10k-events/min burst workload — what are the trade-offs and where should I read up?

id_to_title.doc_b90af0: Reliable Microservices Data Exchange With the Outbox Pattern
id_to_title.doc_92fbe4: Best practices for delegating to Devin
id_to_title.doc_eb2ccf: Practices for Governing Agentic AI Systems
id_to_title.doc_097a73: Implementing Stripe-like idempotency keys in Postgres

excerpts.purpose: Explains why a DB-backed “outbox”/queue pattern is often used to safely bridge state changes and asynchronous delivery (relevant when pairing Postgres with a queue-like table).
excerpts.excerpt: The outbox pattern describes an approach for letting services execute these two tasks in a safe and consistent manner; it provides source services with instant "read your own writes" semantics, while offering reliable, eventually consistent data exchange across service boundaries.

excerpts.purpose: Shows the concrete mechanism of the outbox table: emitting the event record in the same DB transaction as the state change (useful to compare against Postgres SKIP LOCKED claiming rows).
excerpts.excerpt: The idea of this approach is to have an "outbox" table in the service’s database. When receiving a request for placing a purchase order, not only an `INSERT` into the `PurchaseOrder` table is done, but, as part of the same transaction, also a record representing the event to be sent is inserted into that outbox table.

excerpts.purpose: Describes the worker-pool motivation and why durable queues exist: push work to consumers and let many workers share tasks—core to the SQS comparison for burst handling.
excerpts.excerpt: The main idea behind Work Queues (aka: _Task Queues_) is to avoid doing a resource-intensive task immediately and having to wait for it to complete. We encapsulate a _task_ as a message and send it to the queue. A worker process running in the background will pop the tasks and eventually execute the job. When you run many workers the tasks will be shared between them.

excerpts.purpose: Highlights at-least-once/retry behavior under consumer failure (key for designing idempotency for both SQS and DB-claim strategies).
excerpts.excerpt: If a consumer dies (its channel is closed, connection is closed, or TCP connection is lost) without sending an ack, RabbitMQ will understand that a message wasn't processed fully and will re-queue it. If there are other consumers online at the same time, it will then quickly redeliver it to another consumer.

excerpts.purpose: Provides an explicit warning against using a database as the broker under high concurrency/polling—directly relevant to the trade-off of Postgres SKIP LOCKED as a “queue in a table”.
excerpts.excerpt: A database is not built for doing the things a proper AMQP broker like RabbitMQ is designed for. It will break down at one point, probably in production with not that much traffic/user base.

excerpts.purpose: Further details the failure mode: excessive polling/worker concurrency can overload the DB and impact the user-facing workload—central to burst trade-offs.
excerpts.excerpt: Suddenly your database starts falling apart due to the huge number of workers polling the database for new tasks, your disk IO goes through the roof and your webapp starts being affected by this slow down because the workers are basically DDOS-ing the database.

excerpts.purpose: Offers a correctness/safety lens: Jepsen explicitly targets queues and finds issues like lock conflicts and data loss under faults, informing how to think about failure modes (important for both approaches).
excerpts.excerpt: Since 2013, Jepsen has analyzed over two dozen databases, coordination services, and queues—and we’ve found replica divergence, data loss, stale reads, read skew, lock conflicts, and much more.

excerpts.purpose: Emphasizes testing under failure modes (partial failure, clock skew, faulty networks), which is exactly where queue semantics (visibility/leases, retries) get tricky.
excerpts.excerpt: Testing under distributed systems failure modes: faulty networks, unsynchronized clocks, and partial failure. Many test suites only evaluate the behavior of healthy clusters, but production systems experience pathological failure modes. Jepsen shows behavior under strain.

further_reading.blurb: AWS’s SQS best-practices page is the most direct place to review visibility timeout, polling, idempotency, and throughput tuning.
The SQS dead-letter queue (DLQ) doc is useful for designing retry exhaustion and inspecting poison-message handling.
If you’ll route/transform events, Cribl Stream docs can help with operational patterns for high-throughput pipelines (monitoring, processing, routing), complementing queue design.
SRE principles (SLOs/error budgets/on-call) help you decide what failure modes you can tolerate for burst workloads and how to set alerting/guardrails.

gaps.concept: Postgres-specific semantics and operational trade-offs for SKIP LOCKED (claiming rows, lock contention, fairness, and how to build a robust lease/retry loop).
tried_queries: Postgres FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED PostgreSQL documentation
Postgres SKIP LOCKED deadlocks competing consumers work queue table pattern
SELECT FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED PostgreSQL documentation
gaps.needed: PostgreSQL documentation/manpages and/or a deep-dive article describing SELECT ... FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED worker patterns (lease columns, retry/backoff, and correctness properties).

citations.gap_concept: Postgres-specific semantics and operational trade-offs for SKIP LOCKED (claiming rows, lock contention, fairness, and how to build a robust lease/retry loop).
citations.excerpt: With SKIP LOCKED, any selected rows that cannot be immediately locked are skipped. Skipping locked rows provides an unfair view of the data, so this is not suitable for general purpose work, but can be used to avoid lock contention among multiple consumers accessing a queue-like table.
citations.rationale: Official PostgreSQL documentation for the SELECT statement, specifically detailing the Locking Clause behavior.

authors.author: gemini-3-flash-preview · think=low
authors.output:
Use SQS for 10k events/min bursts if you need to protect your primary database from IO spikes, but stick with Postgres `SKIP LOCKED` if you need strict atomic consistency between your data changes and your event emissions.

At 10,000 events per minute, the primary risk of using Postgres as a queue is "polling debt"—where a fleet of workers checking for new rows can effectively DDoS your database. As [Celery Best Practices](https://denibertovic.com/posts/celery-best-practices/) warns, a database isn't a broker; high-concurrency polling can push disk IO through the roof and slow down the user-facing app sharing that same instance. However, Postgres allows you to implement the [Outbox Pattern](https://debezium.io/blog/2019/02/19/reliable-microservices-data-exchange-with-the-outbox-pattern/), where you insert the "work" into a table in the same transaction as your business logic, ensuring you never lose an event if the process crashes mid-request.

SQS is built for this scale and handles the "worker share" logic—distributing tasks across consumers and managing retries—without any configuration overhead. The trade-off is the "dual-write" problem: if your database update succeeds but the SQS push fails, your system becomes inconsistent. If you go the SQS route, you'll need to design for [at-least-once delivery](https://www.rabbitmq.com/tutorials/tutorial-two-python) by making your consumers idempotent, as SQS (especially standard queues) can occasionally deliver the same message twice.

```sdanswer
{
  "type": "tradeoff",
  "title": "Postgres vs. Managed SQS",
  "options": [
    {
      "label": "Postgres SKIP LOCKED",
      "bullets": [
        "Atomic consistency (Outbox pattern)",
        "Zero new infra to manage",
        "Risk of DB contention during bursts",
        "Manual lease/retry logic needed"
      ]
    },
    {
      "label": "Managed SQS",
      "bullets": [
        "Infinite horizontal scale",
        "Built-in retries and DLQs",
        "Potential for duplicate messages",
        "Requires idempotent consumers"
      ]
    }
  ]
}
```

If you choose Postgres, use [SELECT ... FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/current/sql-select.html) to allow multiple workers to claim rows without blocking each other. This turns a standard table into a high-performance work queue, though it provides an "unfair" view of the data—meaning rows aren't strictly processed in order, which is usually fine for background tasks.

Before you commit to Postgres, check if your burst volume is likely to grow; once you hit the limits of a single DB's IOPS, migrating a high-volume queue to a dedicated broker like SQS is significantly more painful than starting there.

prompt: What's the right way to think about transactional outbox vs change data capture for an agentic email platform on Kubernetes?

id_to_title.doc_4082d8: Reliable Microservices Data Exchange With the Outbox Pattern
id_to_title.doc_4adf19: Transactionally Staged Job Drains in Postgres
id_to_title.doc_8b997a: Inversion of Control Containers and the Dependency Injection pattern
id_to_title.doc_064f03: Dependency Injection Principles, Practices, and Patterns

excerpts.purpose: Defines why outbox exists: atomic DB update + reliable publication without 2PC.
excerpts.excerpt:
A service command typically needs to create/update/delete [aggregates] in the database and send messages/events to a message broker.

The command must atomically update the database and send messages in order to avoid data inconsistencies and bugs. However, it is not viable to use a traditional distributed transaction (2PC) that spans the database and the message broker.

excerpts.purpose: States the core outbox mechanism: store the outgoing event in the same DB transaction, then relay asynchronously.
excerpts.excerpt: The solution is for the service that sends the message to first store the message in the database as part of the transaction that updates the business entities. A separate process then sends the messages to the message broker.

excerpts.purpose: Explains the dual-write inconsistency problem and how the outbox balances 'read your own writes' with reliable async propagation.
excerpts.excerpt:
Simply issuing these two requests may lead to potential inconsistencies, though. The reason being that we cannot have one shared transaction that would span the service’s database as well as Apache Kafka, as the latter doesn’t support to be enlisted in distributed (XA) transactions.

Now how would things look like when only writing to the database synchronously and driving the export of a message to Apache Kafka based on that? This is where the outbox pattern comes in.

The idea of this approach is to have an "outbox" table in the service’s database. When receiving a request for placing a purchase order, not only an `INSERT` into the `PurchaseOrder` table is done, but, as part of the same transaction, also a record representing the event to be sent is inserted into that outbox table.

excerpts.purpose: Shows how a CDC tool (Debezium) can be used in the outbox pipeline by routing outbox-table changes into Kafka topics.
excerpts.excerpt: _Update (13 Sept. 2019):_ To simplify usage of the outbox pattern, Debezium now provides a ready-to-use [SMT for routing outbox events](https://debezium.io/documentation/reference/0.9/configuration/outbox-event-router.html). The custom SMT discussed in this blog post is not needed any longer.

excerpts.purpose: Gives an implementation analogy for 'don’t fire background work until the transaction commits'—relevant to outbox relay/email job dispatch.
excerpts.excerpt:
It’s not easy to spot, but if your queue is fast, the job enqueued by `queue_job()` is likely to fail. A worker starts running it before its enclosing transaction is committed, and it fails to access data that it expected to be available.

…

With this pattern, jobs aren’t immediately sent to the job queue. Instead, they’re staged in a table within the relational database itself, and the ACID properties of the running transaction keep them invisible until they’re ready to be worked.

excerpts.purpose: Clarifies the broader design stance: scalable systems typically avoid distributed transactions and instead rely on message-delivery patterns.
excerpts.excerpt: Instead, applications are built using different techniques which do not provide the same transactional guarantees but still meet the needs of their businesses. This paper explores and names some of the practical approaches used in the implementations of large-scale mission-critical applications in a world which rejects distributed transactions.

excerpts.purpose: Provides a contrast point: event sourcing treats changes as an event log (different from 'CDC for integration' or 'outbox for reliable commands').
excerpts.excerpt: Event Sourcing ensures that all changes to application state are stored as a sequence of events.

further_reading.blurb: Kubernetes production best-practices checklist can help you operationalize the outbox relay/worker components (resources, observability, rollout strategy).
Sagas are often the next step after outbox for long-running, multi-step agentic workflows (e.g., compose → consent → send → retries/compensation).
Event sourcing is a related but distinct persistence model; reading it alongside outbox/CDC helps decide what should be the system of record.

gaps.concept: How change data capture (CDC) connectors (e.g., transaction-log-based streaming) differ from outbox semantics for correctness guarantees, ordering, and idempotency in an agentic workflow
tried_queries: transactional outbox vs change data capture CDC Kubernetes agentic email platform
change data capture CDC Kubernetes event streaming
transaction log tailing pattern outbox CDC Debezium polling publisher curated document_id
gaps.needed: A curated document that directly compares outbox vs CDC/transaction-log tailing (not just implementing one) with guidance on when to use each for reliable command-driven workflows.

citations.gap_concept: How change data capture (CDC) connectors (e.g., transaction-log-based streaming) differ from outbox semantics for correctness guarantees, ordering, and idempotency in an agentic workflow
citations.source_title: Reliable Microservices Data Exchange With the Outbox Pattern
citations.excerpt: The idea of this approach is to have an 'outbox' table in the service’s database. When receiving a request... not only an INSERT into the PurchaseOrder table is done, but, as part of the same transaction, also a record representing the event to be sent is inserted into that outbox table... An asynchronous process monitors that table for new entries. If there are any, it propagates the events as messages to Apache Kafka. This gives us a very nice balance of characteristics: By synchronously writing to the PurchaseOrder table, the source service benefits from 'read your own writes' semantics... At the same time, we get reliable, asynchronous, eventually consistent data propagation to other services via Apache Kafka.
citations.rationale: Official Debezium (Red Hat) documentation comparing the trade-offs of the Outbox pattern versus standard CDC log-tailing for reliable event-driven architectures.

authors.author: gemini-3-flash-preview · think=low
authors.output:
Use the **Transactional Outbox pattern** if you need to control the exact shape of your events; use **Change Data Capture (CDC)** as the mechanism to move those events from your database to your message broker. For an agentic email platform, this combination ensures your agents can "read their own writes" while guaranteeing that every sent email or state change is eventually published without the fragility of distributed transactions.

The core problem you're solving is the "dual-write" inconsistency: if you update your database and then try to hit an email API or Kafka, one will eventually fail while the other succeeds. [Pattern: Transactional outbox](https://microservices.io/patterns/data/transactional-outbox.html) solves this by making the "send" part of the local database transaction. You write your business data and a corresponding event record into an `outbox` table in one atomic step. This is especially critical for agents that might trigger multiple downstream actions based on a single prompt or state change.

While you can write a custom poller to tail that table, [Reliable Microservices Data Exchange With the Outbox Pattern](https://debezium.io/blog/2019/02/19/reliable-microservices-data-exchange-with-the-outbox-pattern/) explains why using a CDC tool like Debezium is the more robust choice for the "relay" portion. CDC reads the database's transaction log directly, which is more efficient than polling and ensures you don't miss deletes or rapid updates.

```sdanswer
{
  "type": "tradeoff",
  "title": "Outbox vs. Raw CDC",
  "options": [
    {
      "label": "Transactional Outbox",
      "bullets": [
        "Explicit event schemas",
        "Apps control what is emitted",
        "Avoids leaking DB internals",
        "Requires app-level changes"
      ]
    },
    {
      "label": "Raw CDC",
      "bullets": [
        "Zero app-code changes",
        "Captures every state change",
        "Leaks private table shapes",
        "Harder to version events"
      ]
    }
  ]
}
```

On Kubernetes, you'll likely run your CDC relay (like Debezium) as a separate deployment or via a managed connector. This keeps your agents focused on business logic while the infrastructure handles the "at-least-once" delivery guarantees to your message broker. If your agents are performing long-running tasks—like retrying email sends across several days—look into [Sagas](https://www.cs.cornell.edu/andru/cs711/2002fa/reading/sagas.pdf) as the architectural next step for managing those multi-stage flows.

Ensure your downstream consumers (the services actually hitting the SMTP servers) are idempotent, as the outbox pattern guarantees delivery but can occasionally result in duplicate messages during a relay restart.

citations.gap_concept: Write-ahead logging (WAL) and crash recovery internals (redo/undo records, checkpoints)
citations.source_title: 15-445/645 Database Systems (Fall 2019) - Lecture Notes - 21 ARIES Database Crash Recovery Algorithms
citations.excerpt: The ARIES protocol is comprised of three phases. Upon start-up after a crash, the DBMS will execute the following phases: 1. Analysis: Read the WAL to identify dirty pages in the buffer pool and active transactions at the time of the crash. 2. Redo: Repeat all actions starting from an appropriate point in the log. 3. Undo: Reverse the actions of transactions that did not commit before the crash.
citations.rationale: The CMU 15-445/645 (Database Systems) lecture notes by Andy Pavlo are a canonical academic resource detailing the ARIES recovery algorithm, which is the foundational logic for WAL and crash recovery in modern relational databases.

citations.gap_concept: MVCC implementation details (snapshots, visibility rules, transaction IDs)
citations.source_title: MySQL :: MySQL 8.4 Reference Manual :: 17.3 InnoDB Multi-Versioning
citations.excerpt: InnoDB adds three fields to each row stored in the database: A 6-byte DB_TRX_ID field indicates the transaction identifier for the last transaction that inserted or updated the row. ... A 7-byte DB_ROLL_PTR field called the roll pointer. The roll pointer points to an undo log record written to the undo log. If the row was updated, the undo log record contains the information necessary to rebuild the content of the row before it was updated.
citations.rationale: The official MySQL Reference Manual provides a primary-source deep dive into how InnoDB implements MVCC using hidden system columns (transaction IDs and roll pointers) and undo logs to provide consistent reads.

citations.gap_concept: OpenTelemetry context propagation mechanics for async/await tool calls (so trace/span context isn’t lost between agent iterations)
citations.excerpt: Context is a propagation mechanism which carries execution-scoped values across API boundaries and between logically associated execution units. . . Depending on the language, its usage may be either explicit or implicit. . . For example, in Python, the context is stored in a ThreadLocal or ContextVar.
citations.rationale: Official OpenTelemetry specification document defining the core behavior of Context as an immutable propagation mechanism across execution boundaries.

authors.author: gemini-3-flash-preview · think=low
authors.output:
To debug an agent looping through tool calls, you should wrap each iteration in a child span and use **[OpenTelemetry Context Propagation](https://opentelemetry.io/docs/specs/otel/context/)** to keep them tied to the original production trace. By treating each tool execution as a discrete unit of work within a larger trace, you can see the repetition patterns in real-time without losing the request context that triggered the agent.

Start by ensuring your agent's execution loop honors the incoming trace context; in Python, this usually means ensuring your tool calls happen within the same `ContextVar` scope. You want to send this telemetry to a high-cardinality backend like **[Honeycomb](https://docs.honeycomb.io/get-started/)** or **[Better Stack](https://betterstack.com/docs/logs/tracing/)**, where you can group by specific tool names or agent IDs to spot where the logic is failing. If you're running on Kubernetes and want to see the underlying network calls without manual code changes, **[Pixie](https://docs.px.dev/about-pixie/what-is-pixie/)** can use eBPF to capture those tool-internal HTTP requests automatically.

```sdanswer
{ "type": "path", "links": [
  { "url": "https://opentelemetry.io/docs/specs/otel/context/",
    "description": "Understand how context is carried across execution units so your agent's loops stay attached to the parent trace.",
    "estimate": "45m" },
  { "url": "https://betterstack.com/docs/logs/tracing/",
    "description": "Learn to correlate spans with logs and metrics so you can see exactly what the LLM was thinking during each repeated call.",
    "estimate": "1h" },
  { "url": "https://docs.honeycomb.io/get-started/",
    "description": "Use high-cardinality fields like 'tool_input' or 'token_count' to filter and find the specific iteration that broke.",
    "estimate": "1h 30m" },
  { "url": "https://logfire.pydantic.dev/docs/",
    "description": "If you're in Python, this is the most direct way to get structured LLM-aware spans without fighting raw OTel SDKs.",
    "estimate": "45m" }
] }
```

Once you have the trace data, look for "depth" vs "breadth" in your spans—a loop often manifests as a flat line of dozens of identical peer spans under a single parent, which is your clearest signal to implement a "max iterations" kill switch.
